_doc.what: SYNTHETIC voice corpus for the voice-onboard extraction eval. Five hand-authored samples attributed to a FICTIONAL author (Mara Quill). This is NOT a real author's writing and must never be described as one.
_doc.why: voice-onboard is skill prose: an agent reads samples and writes a voice profile. There is no function to unit-test. So the corpus is engineered with traits whose ground truth is countable by hand, and the eval scores PROFILE ARTIFACTS (see voice_profile_good.md / voice_profile_bad.md) against that ground truth. Every check has a planted positive: the bad profile must trip it.
engineered_traits: HABIT word 'ledger' appears exactly 4x -> clears the SKILL's 3+ rule -> a correct profile learns it.
NOISE word 'kaleidoscope' appears exactly 2x -> fails the 3+ rule -> a correct profile must NOT learn it. The SKILL calls this 'the single most common extraction mistake'.
ABSENT words 'delve' and 'leverage' appear 0x -> per the SKILL, absences are the strongest signal -> a correct profile lists them under Vocabulary Don't.
BOUNDARY DECOY 'ledgers' appears exactly 1x -> must not inflate the 'ledger' count (word-boundary counting).
MIXED GENRES: 3 terse tweets vs 2 long memos, with genuinely different rhythm -> a profile that averages them into one flat sentence-length figure is detectably wrong; a correct one splits by genre.
_doc.ground_truth_discipline: The counts below are DECLARED. run_voice_extraction.py RECOMPUTES them from sample text at runtime and fails loudly on any disagreement. If they ever disagree, fix the SAMPLES, not the declared numbers - a fixture whose own ground truth has drifted is worse than no fixture.

habit_word.rule: 3+ occurrences -> habit -> MUST appear in the profile

noise_word.rule: <3 occurrences -> noise -> MUST NOT appear in the profile

boundary_decoy.rule: plural must NOT be counted as an occurrence of 'ledger'

samples.text: Shipped the pricing ledger today. Two weeks late. It works.
Every forecast is a story. The ledger is the fact. Trust the ledger.
Dashboards are a kaleidoscope. Nobody reads them. Cut three.

The revenue ledger we maintain in finance is the only artifact in this company that has never lied to us, and I would rather ship a forecast late than ship one that disagrees with it. When the quarterly review turns into a kaleidoscope of competing dashboards, each owned by a different team and each telling a slightly flattering story, the honest move is to go back to the one number nobody is allowed to edit. I am not asking anyone to stop building dashboards, but I am asking that every dashboard we present next quarter carries a line showing where its numbers diverge from finance.

Our onboarding funnel lost eleven percent of new accounts last month, and the cause was not the product but a queue that silently dropped retries after midnight. I spent a day reading the raw event logs instead of the summary tables, because summary tables round away exactly the kind of small persistent failure that ends up costing a quarter. The fix is boring: retry with backoff, alert on queue depth, and reconcile the two ledgers every morning before anyone looks at a chart.
